content.text: Why Claude Integration Code Needs a Different Refactoring Discipline

Refactoring conventional code is safe when it satisfies one criterion: the observable behaviour of the system remains unchanged. For Claude integration code, that criterion is harder to verify. The 'behaviour' of an AI call is its output distribution — not a fixed return value but a statistical property across a range of inputs. A structural change that looks neutral (renaming a variable, extracting a function, moving retry logic) can inadvertently change the prompt, the model parameters, or the tool definitions in ways that shift that distribution.

The core discipline is the separation invariant: structural changes (how the code is organised) and behavioural changes (what the model receives) must never happen in the same commit. A refactor that inadvertently changes model parameters is not a refactor — it is an unreviewed behaviour change that will not have a corresponding eval result.

This sub-subtopic defines the safe refactoring protocol for Claude integration code, the structural vs. behavioural change taxonomy, common refactoring mistakes that silently alter AI behaviour, the eval-based verification step that replaces 'does it compile?' for AI code, and a testing pattern that makes accidental parameter changes detectable before they reach production.
The Safe Refactoring Protocol

attrs.alt: Safe refactoring protocol: Snapshot → Isolate → Refactor → Verify → commit or revert

content.text: Four steps before committing any refactor to Claude integration code:
Snapshot the baseline

: run the eval suite and record the pass rate and median latency. This is the bar the refactored version must match. Do not skip this step even if the refactor 'clearly' does not touch the AI logic — the point is to detect unintended changes.
Isolate the AI call

: before restructuring the surrounding code, extract the Claude API call into a pure function with named parameters. This makes it impossible to accidentally change model parameters while moving code around, because the parameters are now explicit constants with names.
Refactor the structure

: change the organisation of the code — extract functions, rename variables, move error handling. Do not change the prompt content, model identifier, temperature, max_tokens, or tool list in this commit.
Verify against the baseline

: re-run the eval suite. Compare the new pass rate and latency to the baseline snapshot. A regression of more than 1% pass rate, or a latency increase of more than 10%, requires investigation before the refactor is committed. If the results match, commit. If not, revert and investigate.
Structural vs. Behavioural Changes
The separation invariant requires that you correctly classify every change as structural or behavioural before committing it.
Change Type
Category
Safe in Refactor Commit?
Why
Rename variable holding the API response
Structural
Yes
Does not affect what is sent to Claude or how the response is parsed.
Extract the API call into a separate function
Structural
Yes
If the function parameters are named and unchanged.
Move retry logic into a decorator
Structural
Yes
Retry behaviour does not affect prompt or output.
Change the system prompt text
Behavioural
No
Directly changes model output distribution.
Change temperature from 0.7 to 0.5
Behavioural
No
Changes output variance — must be a deliberate, reviewed change.
Add a tool to the available tools list
Behavioural
No
Changes what actions the model can take.
Change max_tokens from 1024 to 2048
Behavioural
No
May change output length distribution; affects cost.
Change the model from haiku to sonnet
Behavioural
No
Different capability profile; must go through eval gate.
Change error handling around the API call
Structural
Yes
How errors are handled does not change what Claude receives.
Reorder messages in the messages array
Behavioural
No
Instruction order affects model attention; treat as a prompt change.
Isolating the AI Call: The Named-Parameter Pattern

The most effective way to prevent accidental parameter changes during a refactor is to isolate all model parameters into a named constant or configuration object before starting the refactor. This makes any change to model parameters visible as a diff on the constant, rather than hidden inside a function call:
Before isolation (fragile to refactoring)

import anthropic

def classify_ticket(ticket_text: str) -> dict:
    """Classify a support ticket using Claude."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        temperature=0.0,
        system="Classify the following support ticket into one of: billing, technical, account, other. Return JSON.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": ticket_text}],
    )
    import json
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

If this function is refactored by extracting the API call into a helper, it is easy to accidentally move the parameters into the wrong place, change their defaults, or omit one. The refactor leaves no explicit signal that the model parameters are sensitive.
After isolation (explicit and refactor-safe)

import anthropic, json
from dataclasses import dataclass

# All model parameters in one named, visible constant.
# A refactor that touches this block is a behavioural change, not a structural one.
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TicketClassifierParams:
    model: str          = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
    max_tokens: int     = 256
    temperature: float  = 0.0
    system_prompt: str  = (
        "Classify the following support ticket into one of: "
        "billing, technical, account, other. Return JSON."
    )

_PARAMS = TicketClassifierParams()

def _call_claude(ticket_text: str, params: TicketClassifierParams) -> str:
    """
    Pure function: send ticket_text to Claude and return the raw response text.
    Separated so it can be mocked in unit tests without a real API call.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=params.model,
        max_tokens=params.max_tokens,
        temperature=params.temperature,
        system=params.system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": ticket_text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def classify_ticket(ticket_text: str) -> dict:
    """
    Classify a support ticket. Structural changes to this function
    (error handling, retries, logging) are safe. Parameter changes
    require a separate commit against _PARAMS.
    """
    raw = _call_claude(ticket_text, _PARAMS)
    return json.loads(raw)

Now any change to the model identifier, temperature, max_tokens, or system prompt is a change to _PARAMS — which appears as its own diff hunk in code review, is covered by a snapshot test (see below), and triggers the CODEOWNERS rule on the prompts/ directory.
Snapshot Tests for Model Parameters

A snapshot test records the exact value of the model parameters at a point in time and fails if they change. This is different from an eval test (which tests output quality) — it tests the configuration of the call itself. Any change to the parameters breaks the snapshot and forces the developer to explicitly update it, producing a visible diff in the PR:

# tests/test_ticket_classifier_params.py
"""
Snapshot tests for TicketClassifierParams.

These tests fail if model parameters change without a deliberate update.
When a parameter change is intentional, update the expected values here
in the same commit as the parameter change, and note the reason in the
commit message body.
"""
import unittest
from src.ticket_classifier import TicketClassifierParams, _PARAMS

class TestTicketClassifierParamsSnapshot(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_model_identifier(self):
        """
        Model changes alter cost and capability profile.
        Changing this requires an eval suite run before commit.
        """
        self.assertEqual(_PARAMS.model, "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

    def test_max_tokens(self):
        """
        Increasing max_tokens raises cost and may change output length
        distribution. Decreasing it may truncate valid responses.
        """
        self.assertEqual(_PARAMS.max_tokens, 256)

    def test_temperature(self):
        """
        Temperature 0.0 gives deterministic outputs for a given input,
        which is required for the eval suite to produce stable results.
        Any change to temperature must be evaluated against all eval cases.
        """
        self.assertEqual(_PARAMS.temperature, 0.0)

    def test_system_prompt_not_empty(self):
        self.assertTrue(len(_PARAMS.system_prompt) > 0)

    def test_system_prompt_contains_output_format_instruction(self):
        """
        The output format instruction (Return JSON) is load-bearing:
        the JSON parser in classify_ticket() depends on it. If this
        instruction is removed, classify_ticket() will raise.
        """
        self.assertIn("Return JSON", _PARAMS.system_prompt)

    def test_system_prompt_contains_all_categories(self):
        """
        All four categories must be in the prompt. Removing one would
        cause the classifier to never return that category, silently
        mis-classifying tickets.
        """
        for category in ["billing", "technical", "account", "other"]:
            self.assertIn(category, _PARAMS.system_prompt,
                f"Category '{category}' missing from classifier prompt")
Eval-Gated Refactoring in CI
The four-step protocol described above is enforced in CI by running the eval suite on every PR that touches the integration code, not just on prompt-change PRs:

# .github/workflows/refactoring-gate.yml
name: Refactoring gate — eval verification

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**/*.py'       # any Python change to integration code
      - 'tools/**/*.json'   # any tool schema change

jobs:
  snapshot-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
      - name: Run parameter snapshot tests
        run: python -m pytest tests/test_ticket_classifier_params.py -v

  eval-verification:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: snapshot-tests
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
      - name: Run eval suite and compare to baseline
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: python scripts/eval_gate.py --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier.json

# scripts/eval_gate.py
"""
Run the eval suite and compare the result to a stored baseline.
Exits with code 1 if pass_rate drops by more than REGRESSION_THRESHOLD.

Usage:
    python scripts/eval_gate.py --baseline evals/baselines/ticket_classifier.json
"""
import argparse, json, sys
from pathlib import Path

REGRESSION_THRESHOLD = 0.01   # 1% drop in pass rate triggers a failure

def load_baseline(path: Path) -> dict:
    """Load the stored baseline eval result from a JSON file."""
    with path.open() as f:
        return json.load(f)

def run_eval_suite() -> dict:
    """
    Run the full eval suite against the current code and return
    a result dict with pass_rate and per_case_results.
    """
    # Import here to avoid loading model code at module level.
    from evals.runner import run_suite
    return run_suite("evals/ticket_classifier/")

def check_regression(baseline: dict, current: dict) -> list[str]:
    """
    Compare current results to baseline. Return a list of failure
    messages if regression exceeds the threshold.
    """
    failures = []
    baseline_rate = baseline.get("pass_rate", 1.0)
    current_rate = current.get("pass_rate", 0.0)
    delta = baseline_rate - current_rate

    if delta > REGRESSION_THRESHOLD:
        failures.append(
            f"pass_rate regressed by {delta:.1%}: "
            f"baseline={baseline_rate:.2%} current={current_rate:.2%}"
        )
    return failures

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--baseline", required=True, type=Path)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    baseline = load_baseline(args.baseline)
    current = run_eval_suite()

    failures = check_regression(baseline, current)
    if failures:
        for f in failures:
            print(f"FAIL: {f}")
        print("\nThe refactoring caused a regression. Revert or investigate before committing.")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print(f"OK: pass_rate={current['pass_rate']:.2%} (baseline={baseline['pass_rate']:.2%})")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Common Refactoring Mistakes
Mistake
How It Happens
Consequence
Prevention
Inlining a constant into the function call
Removing a named constant to simplify the code; the value is now an inline literal
The value becomes invisible to snapshot tests and code review
Never inline model parameters; always keep them in a named dataclass or config object
Reordering messages during a cleanup
Moving the system message from the system parameter to the first message in the messages array
Some models handle system messages differently when placed in the messages array
Snapshot test that asserts the system param is used, not the messages array
Changing the model string format
Normalising the model ID by removing a version suffix (claude-haiku instead of claude-haiku-4-5-20251001)
Routes to a different model; capability and cost change silently
Snapshot test on the exact model string including version suffix
Merging a structural and behavioural change
While refactoring, also 'fixing' the temperature to a lower value
The eval regression is attributed to the refactor, masking the parameter change
Run evals on the structural change first; make parameter changes in a follow-up commit
Modifying the prompt in a docstring
Updating a code comment that happens to be the source of truth for the prompt
The comment and the actual prompt diverge; future developers use the wrong source
Prompts must never live in code comments; always load from a file in prompts/
Refactoring Retry and Timeout Logic
Retry and timeout logic around the API call is structural — it does not change what Claude receives — but it is easy to write incorrectly in ways that affect AI behaviour indirectly. Specifically:
Retrying on non-transient errors
: if the retry logic retries on a 400 Bad Request (invalid request body), each retry sends the same malformed prompt repeatedly. This wastes quota and masks the real error.
Truncating context on retry

: some retry implementations reduce max_tokens or truncate the messages array to fit within a tighter context budget. This changes what Claude receives and is a behavioural change, not a structural one.
Changing timeout mid-conversation
: if a multi-turn conversation times out and the retry uses a different timeout value, the model may receive truncated history on the retry.
The correct pattern is to retry only on transient errors (429 rate limit, 529 overloaded, 5xx server errors) and to preserve the exact parameters on every retry attempt:

# src/api_client.py
"""
Retry wrapper for Claude API calls.
Retries only on transient errors; never modifies the request parameters.
"""
import time, anthropic
from anthropic import APIStatusError, APITimeoutError, APIConnectionError

# Transient status codes that warrant a retry.
TRANSIENT_STATUS_CODES = {429, 529, 500, 502, 503, 504}

def call_with_retry(
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
    max_retries: int = 3,
    initial_backoff: float = 1.0,
    **kwargs,
) -> anthropic.types.Message:
    """
    Call client.messages.create(**kwargs) with exponential backoff.
    The kwargs are passed through unchanged on every attempt —
    no parameter modification on retry.

    Args:
        client: Anthropic client instance.
        max_retries: Maximum number of retry attempts after the first try.
        initial_backoff: Seconds to wait before the first retry.
        **kwargs: Passed verbatim to client.messages.create().
    """
    last_error: Exception | None = None
    for attempt in range(max_retries + 1):
        try:
            return client.messages.create(**kwargs)
        except APIStatusError as e:
            if e.status_code not in TRANSIENT_STATUS_CODES:
                # Non-transient error (400, 401, 403) — do not retry.
                raise
            last_error = e
        except (APITimeoutError, APIConnectionError) as e:
            last_error = e

        if attempt < max_retries:
            wait = initial_backoff * (2 ** attempt)
            time.sleep(wait)

    raise RuntimeError(f"All {max_retries + 1} attempts failed") from last_error
Refactoring Multi-Step Agents
Single-call integration code is relatively straightforward to refactor safely. Multi-step agents — where the output of one Claude call feeds into another — require additional care because:
A structural change to the orchestration layer (how steps are chained) can accidentally change the context passed to each step.
Moving shared context out of a closure into a dataclass can change how it is serialised and passed to the next step.
Extracting a step into a separate function may change the order of messages if the refactor fails to thread conversation history correctly.
The safest approach for multi-step agents is to write integration tests that capture the complete message sequence sent to Claude at each step, not just the final output:

# tests/test_triage_agent_message_sequence.py
"""
Capture the exact message sequence sent to Claude at each agent step.
These tests verify that refactoring the orchestration layer does not
accidentally change the context passed to the model.
"""
import unittest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, call, patch
from src.triage_agent import run_triage_pipeline

# Expected message sequence for a 2-step triage pipeline:
# Step 1: classify the ticket
# Step 2: draft a response given the classification
EXPECTED_STEP_1_MESSAGES = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "My invoice shows a double charge for June."}
]
EXPECTED_STEP_2_SYSTEM = (
    "You are a customer support writer. The ticket has been classified as 'billing'. "
    "Draft a polite, concise response."
)

class TestTriageAgentMessageSequence(unittest.TestCase):

    def setUp(self):
        self.mock_client = MagicMock()
        # Return minimal valid Message objects for each call.
        self.mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = [
            MagicMock(content=[MagicMock(text='{"category": "billing"}')]),
            MagicMock(content=[MagicMock(text="Thank you for contacting us about your invoice.")]),
        ]

    @patch("src.triage_agent.anthropic.Anthropic")
    def test_step1_receives_correct_messages(self, mock_anthropic_cls):
        mock_anthropic_cls.return_value = self.mock_client
        run_triage_pipeline("My invoice shows a double charge for June.")

        first_call_kwargs = self.mock_client.messages.create.call_args_list[0].kwargs
        self.assertEqual(first_call_kwargs["messages"], EXPECTED_STEP_1_MESSAGES)

    @patch("src.triage_agent.anthropic.Anthropic")
    def test_step2_receives_classification_in_system_prompt(self, mock_anthropic_cls):
        mock_anthropic_cls.return_value = self.mock_client
        run_triage_pipeline("My invoice shows a double charge for June.")

        second_call_kwargs = self.mock_client.messages.create.call_args_list[1].kwargs
        self.assertEqual(second_call_kwargs["system"], EXPECTED_STEP_2_SYSTEM)

    @patch("src.triage_agent.anthropic.Anthropic")
    def test_exactly_two_api_calls_made(self, mock_anthropic_cls):
        mock_anthropic_cls.return_value = self.mock_client
        run_triage_pipeline("My invoice shows a double charge for June.")

        # A refactor that merges or splits steps would change this count.
        self.assertEqual(self.mock_client.messages.create.call_count, 2)

These tests pin the message sequence at each step. When a structural refactor runs, they fail if the messages change — making any accidental context modification immediately visible. When an intentional change is made (adding a new step, changing what context is passed), the tests are updated in the same commit as the change, creating an explicit record of what was changed and why.
Summary

Refactoring Claude integration code safely requires the separation invariant: structural changes (how the code is organised) and behavioural changes (what the model receives) must never happen in the same commit. The isolated parameter pattern — concentrating all model parameters in a named dataclass — makes accidental changes visible as explicit diffs. Snapshot tests on the parameter values prevent silent regressions. For multi-step agents, message-sequence tests capture the full context passed to Claude at each step, catching structural refactors that accidentally change what the model receives. The four-step protocol (snapshot baseline → isolate the AI call → refactor structure → verify against baseline) replaces 'does it compile?' with 'does it produce the same quality outputs?' as the gate for committing a refactor.
